body_txt: Have you noticed that some characters have different origins when different people are writing the story? Sometimes this is done on purpose, to give an air of Unreliable Narrator . Sometimes it's caused by repeated retcons . Sometimes it's just because the writers got it wrong, sometimes the result of a deliberate Retcon , rewrite or by creators Armed with Canon . This trope is particularly common in comic books, as a single character may be written by dozens of writers over their history. Sometimes, as evidenced in the Trope Namer , it is also done in order to depict the character giving their backstory as being too insane to give a true account of their past, believing every single account they gave about themselves; or to cultivate an air of personal mystery , a common play by a Consummate Liar who wants to remain Shrouded in Myth . Having messed-up memories due to a case of Laser-Guided Amnesia that isn't so laser-guided , Trauma-Induced Amnesia , and/or implantation of Fake Memories also works just fine. Video Games occasionally invoke this as part of a starting conversation to decide what your basic character build will be like, which may be the first part of Story Branching . Other games make you play through one of several origin levels before the story proper begins — this is known as Multiple Game Openings . Along with multiple authors, this is a feature of the earliest recorded myths, making it Older Than Dirt . Compare Broad Strokes (where a series' stance on canon is that certain previous events still happened, but not necessarily in the exact way the events unfolded in the original stories), Comic-Book Time (where characters stay the same age or age at a considerably slower pace than they realistically should in spite of the series going on for years or even decades, which may include discrepancies in the characters' established past happening as a side effect of the floating timeline), Continuity Snarl (where a development or event in the story flagrantly contradicts what was previously established), Depending on the Writer (where a character has discrepancies in their personality, interests and so on as the result of different writers having different perspectives of the character or choosing to interpret the character in a way that best suits the stories they intend to write), Expansion Pack Past (where details added to a character's past lead to it becoming a contradictory mess), Negative Continuity (where a series' continuity lacks internal consistency and therefore any details given about a character's backstory will contradict each other), Schrödinger's Question (where the correct answer to the nature of the player's character in a game is whatever the player wants it to be), Adaptational Backstory Change (where an adaptation gives the character a different origin from what they had in the source material) and Origins Episode (where a character whose origins weren't clarified at the start has an episode devoted to revealing their origins). Sounds similar to but has nothing to do with Multiple-Choice Future . Where alternate (and even contradictory) pasts are actually true, may be an example of Mind Hive or Fusion Dance .
